 {"id":"wa-4vx.2.4","title":"WezTerm interface hardening: socket targeting, retries, and pane lifecycle edge cases","description":"# Task: WezTerm interface hardening

## Goal
Make the interface resilient to common real-world failures:
- WezTerm not running or restarting
- mux socket mismatch
- pane disappearing between list and get-text
- transient command failures

## Deliverables
- Retry strategy for safe operations (get-text/list) with bounded attempts.
- Clear "pane not found" behavior.
- Optional helpers:
  - `get_text_with_fallback` (re-list panes and attempt to re-resolve by fingerprint)

## Testing
- Unit/integration tests (fixture-driven):
  - transient wezterm CLI failures are retried (bounded) and then fail with actionable error
  - pane disappears between list and get-text → stable "pane not found" outcome
  - mux socket mismatch yields actionable remediation
  - retry logic never loops forever

## Acceptance Criteria
- Under simulated failures, wa degrades gracefully (errors are actionable).

 {"id":"wa-4vx.2.5","title":"WezTerm CLI parsing tests + fixture harness","description":"# Task: WezTerm CLI parsing tests + fixture harness

## Goal
Ensure wa detects upstream WezTerm CLI output drift quickly and fails with a clear diagnostic instead of silently mis-parsing.

We depend on `wezterm cli list --format json` (and friends) for core functionality in v0. The parsing layer must be resilient.

## Fixture strategy
- Store a set of `wezterm cli list` JSON fixtures representing:
  - multiple domains
  - varying cwd/title formats
  - panes with missing/empty fields
  - unicode titles and paths

- Fixtures should be treated as a golden corpus:
  - adding a fixture is trivial
  - tests report *which field* broke when upstream changes

## What to validate
- JSON deserialization succeeds.
- Derived fields are correct and stable:
  - domain inference
  - cwd parsing / normalization
  - pane identity/fingerprint rules
- Error messages are actionable:
  - show which fixture failed
  - point to remediation (update fixture / adjust parser)

## Testing
- Unit tests for pure parsing + derivation.
- No dependency on a real wezterm binary (fixtures only).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tests pass on the current fixture corpus.
- A parser regression points to the exact failing fixture + field.
- Adding a new fixture requires minimal ceremony.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.2.6","title":"PaneWaiter utility: shared wait-for (substring/regex) with timeout/backoff","description":"# Task: PaneWaiter (shared wait logic)

## Goal
Provide a single, well-tested implementation for "wait until a pane satisfies a condition" that can be reused everywhere:
- `wa robot send --wait-for` verification
- `wa robot wait-for`
- workflow `WaitCondition::Pattern` and other waits
- E2E harness synchronization points

## Why this matters
Waiting is one of the easiest places to accidentally introduce:
- flakiness (sleep loops, overly short tails, missed matches)
- performance issues (polling too aggressively, reading too much scrollback)
- duplicated logic that diverges across robot/workflow code paths

A shared PaneWaiter makes behavior consistent and debuggable for users.

## Design (v0)
- API: `wait_for(pane_id, matcher, timeout, options) -> WaitResult`
- Matcher types:
  - `Substring(String)` (fast path)
  - `Regex(Regex)` (explicit flag; avoid footguns)
- Text acquisition:
  - uses the same underlying pane text acquisition (WezTerm CLI get-text)
  - configurable `tail_lines` (default small, but enough for common prompts)
  - optional `escapes` mode when required for alt-screen detection

## Polling/backoff
- Bounded polling loop with adaptive backoff:
  - start fast (low latency for interactive flows)
  - backoff to a max interval (avoid CPU churn)
  - always respect timeout/deadline
- Add a guard like `max_polls` to prevent runaway loops in case of clock bugs.

## Cancellation + shutdown
- Must be cancellable (tokio cancellation) so workflows can abort cleanly and `wa watch` shutdown is fast.

## Logging + audit
- Log start/success/timeout at INFO with pane_id + timeout + elapsed.
- Never log full pane content; at most log a short redacted matcher description.
- When used by actions (send/workflow), the caller records verification details into audit trail.

## Testing
- Deterministic unit tests using a fake text source (scripted sequence of tails).
- Timeout behavior: stable error + elapsed_ms.
- Backoff behavior: does not busy-loop and respects deadline.
- Cancellation behavior: aborts quickly when cancelled.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Robot send, robot wait-for, and workflows share this implementation (no duplicated polling loops).
- Unit tests cover success, timeout, cancellation, and backoff bounds.

-{"id":"wa-4vx.2.7","title":"[EPIC] Lua IPC status updates (WezTerm update-status signals)","description":"# [EPIC] Lua IPC status updates (WezTerm update-status signals)

## Mission
Add a low-latency, deterministic lane for pane metadata updates (cursor, dimensions, title, alt-screen) using WezTerm's `update-status` hook.

## Why this matters
CLI polling only gives us output text. For safe automation we also need *current pane state* and *metadata*:
- PromptActive / CommandRunning hints (used for policy and workflow guards)
- Alt-screen status (must never inject input)
- Title + cursor position to interpret agent readiness

`update-status` lets WezTerm report this state without parsing output, improving correctness and reducing guesswork.

## Scope
- Define a small `status_update` JSON schema.
- Use the existing watcher IPC socket to accept `status_update` messages.
- Update in-memory pane state and persist any relevant fields.
- Provide rate limiting / coalescing so status updates do not spam the event bus.

## Non-goals
- Real-time output streaming (handled by vendored streaming in Phase 5).
- Replacing OSC 133 prompt markers (this is additive, not a replacement).

## Deliverables
- Versioned schema for `status_update` payloads.
- Watcher handling that updates pane state deterministically.
- WezTerm Lua snippet support (installed via `wa setup`).
- Tests + E2E case with artifacts.

## Success Criteria
- Pane state is updated even when there is no new output.
- Policy and workflows can rely on the status updates to make safer decisions.
- Logs/artifacts are clear and never include sensitive payloads.

## Testing
- Unit tests for schema validation and coalescing behavior.
- Integration tests for watcher IPC receiving status updates.
- E2E script with real WezTerm (or deterministic stub) that proves updates flow end-to-end with artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All items in the following sections are satisfied: Deliverables.
- All Testing requirements pass (unit/integration/E2E) with the logging/artifacts described above.
- Any explicit dependencies for this bead are implemented and validated via tests or E2E artifacts.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.2.7.1","title":"IPC status_update schema + watcher ingestion (pane state refresh)","description":"# Task: IPC status_update schema + watcher ingestion

## Goal
Define a small, versioned `status_update` payload and teach the watcher to ingest it safely and update pane state.

## Schema (v0)
Minimal, deterministic payload:
- `pane_id` (u64)
- `domain` (string, optional)
- `title` (string, optional, bounded length)
- `cursor` (row/col)
- `dimensions` (cols/rows)
- `is_alt_screen` (bool)
- `is_active` (bool, optional)
- `ts` (unix ms or ISO8601)
- `schema_version` (int)

## Behavior
- Validate size limits and schema_version.
- Coalesce rapid updates (rate-limit per pane).
- **Respect pane selection filters**: if a pane is ignored, drop status updates and avoid persistence.
- Update in-memory pane registry:
  - title, dimensions, cursor
  - alt-screen flag
- Persist fields that matter (if we store them in `panes` table).
- Emit a lightweight internal signal if state changes materially (e.g., alt-screen toggles).

## Safety
- Never log raw payloads by default.
- Treat malformed payloads as non-fatal (drop + structured error).
- If pane is unknown, record a warning and skip (do not create phantom panes).

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - schema validation and size limits
  - rate limiting/coalescing (no event bus spam)
  - alt-screen state transitions are handled deterministically
  - ignored panes do not update state
- Integration tests:
  - send a synthetic status_update into the IPC receiver and assert pane registry updates

## Acceptance Criteria
- Status updates change pane state without requiring output text.
- Alt-screen changes are reflected in `PaneCapabilities` (and policy gates can use them).
- Malformed payloads never crash the watcher.
- Ignored panes do not change state or persist metadata.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.2.7.2","title":"Extend wezterm.lua patcher to emit status_update signals","description":"# Task: Extend wezterm.lua patcher to emit status_update signals

## Goal
Install a safe, idempotent Lua snippet that sends `status_update` payloads from WezTerm to wa.

## Why
The watcher needs low-latency pane metadata without parsing output. `update-status` is the right hook.

## Lua behavior
- Use `wezterm.on('update-status', ...)`.
- Build a minimal JSON payload:
  - pane_id, domain, title, cursor, dimensions, alt-screen
- Send to wa via `wezterm.background_child_process { 'wa', 'event', '--from-status', ... }` (or the agreed event flag).
- Rate-limit per pane (e.g., 1 update per 2s) to avoid spam.

## Idempotent patching
- Extend the existing WA-managed block (`WA-BEGIN/WA-END`).
- Must remain safe if re-run (`wa setup` idempotency test stays green).
- If Lua snippet cannot run (missing wezterm.json), it must fail silently (no impact on terminal UX).

## Testing
- Fixture tests (reuse `wa-nu4.3.3.5`):
  - snippet inserted exactly once
  - re-run is a no-op
- E2E covered by status_update lane test (see new E2E case)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Re-running `wa setup` does not duplicate the snippet.
- The snippet emits `status_update` payloads without blocking the UI.

- Failure artifacts clearly indicate which step broke (Lua emission, IPC, watcher update).

## Testing

  - Ensure the E2E runner captures both wezterm and wa logs with timestamps.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.3","title":"[EPIC] Storage & indexing (SQLite WAL + FTS5 + async writer)","description":"# Storage & indexing — SQLite WAL + FTS5

## Goal
Persist:
- raw pane output as **append-only segments** (delta-first)
- explicit `GAP` events when continuity cannot be guaranteed
- detected events + extracted facts
- workflow execution traces (step logs) for resumability

and provide fast query surfaces:
- full-text search (FTS5) across all captured output
- scoped search per pane/domain/time

## Rationale
The database is wa’s "world memory". It enables:
- debugging (what happened?)
- agent coordination (search + recall)
- workflow correctness (structured facts, resumability)

We choose SQLite because:
- zero-config, embeddable, portable
- supports WAL for concurrent readers + single writer
- FTS5 is excellent for terminal transcript search

## Architectural constraints
- `rusqlite::Connection` is not `Send` and cannot be used directly inside async tasks.
- We must not block the async runtime with DB writes.

Therefore the design is:
- **single dedicated writer thread** receiving bounded write commands
- **small read-only connection pool** for queries (WAL allows concurrent reads)

## Deliverables
- Schema and migrations strategy (even if v0 is "init schema only"):
  - domains, panes
  - output segments (seq monotonic per pane)
  - output gaps (explicit discontinuities)
  - FTS virtual table + triggers
  - events (detections)
  - agent sessions (token usage, session IDs, etc.)
  - workflow executions + step logs
  - accounts + config + maintenance log
- `StorageHandle` async API with:
  - `append_segment`, `record_gap`, `record_event`
  - `search(fts_query, options)` with correct bm25 ranking
  - `workflow_step_*` methods for durable step logs
- Retention + hygiene mechanisms:
  - retention-by-age and/or size
  - explicit `vacuum` command only

## Acceptance
- `wa` can write segments and query them back.
- FTS finds known text quickly.
- Writer thread enforces per-pane seq monotonicity and emits/records gaps when violated.



## Success Criteria
- SQLite schema + migrations exist and are exercised by tests.
- StorageHandle provides single-writer semantics and supports concurrent read queries without blocking the async runtime.
- FTS search works end-to-end (segments written → indexed → searchable) with deterministic scoping.
- Core storage invariants are enforced (seq monotonicity, explicit gaps, audit/event/workflow trace durability).
- Unit/integration tests cover writer queue behavior, migrations, FTS queries, and workflow/audit storage paths.


## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - Schema/migration roundtrips and “invariant checks” (seq monotonicity, explicit gaps, FK consistency).
  - Writer queue behavior under load (bounded queue backpressure, graceful shutdown flush).
  - FTS query correctness and scoping (pane/domain/time) including snippet/highlight determinism.

- Integration tests:
  - End-to-end “write segments → index → search” with deterministic fixtures.
  - Restart/resume: open DB, write, close, reopen, verify writer thread state rehydrates correctly.

- Property/fuzz tests (as additive confidence for correctness):
  - Proptest seq generation ensures monotonic enforcement and correct GAP insertion semantics (`wa-4vx.10.5`).
  - Fuzz inputs for query parsing / escaping / injection-hardening (`wa-4vx.10.3`).

- Artifact/log requirements (for integration/E2E):
  - On failure, dump the minimal DB snapshot or deterministic query output needed to reproduce.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All items in the following sections are satisfied: Success Criteria, Deliverables.
- All Testing requirements pass (unit/integration/E2E) with the logging/artifacts described above.
- Any explicit dependencies for this bead are implemented and validated via tests or E2E artifacts.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.3.1","title":"Define SQLite schema (segments+gaps+events+workflows+FTS) + init/migration strategy","description":"# Task: Define SQLite schema + init/migration strategy

## Goal
Define the database schema that supports:
- append-only output segments (delta-first)
- explicit output gaps
- panes metadata + observation status (observed vs ignored)
- events (detections) with extracted JSON
- event lifecycle: unhandled vs handled, workflow linkage, idempotency/dedupe
- agent sessions and token usage
- workflow executions + step logs (durability/resumability)
- accounts mirror (usage tracking)
- config + maintenance log
- (later) audit trail of actions (see wa-4vx.3.8)

## Rationale
DB schema mistakes are expensive. We want a schema that:
- is append-friendly (write-optimized)
- is easy to query for recent state
- enables FTS for fast recall
- supports workflow resumption after crash
- supports user trust: we can explain why wa acted (events + workflows + audit)

## Required properties
- WAL mode, foreign_keys on.
- output_segments has UNIQUE(pane_id, seq).
- GAPs are explicit (output_gaps).
- FTS5 virtual table over output_segments + triggers.

## Panes table requirements
We need a panes table to support:
- stable UI/state surfaces (wa status / wa robot state)
- observation decisions (privacy/perf)

Minimum fields (names flexible):
- pane_id (and domain/window/tab identifiers)
- title, cwd
- last_seen_at
- observed: bool
- ignore_reason: nullable short string (rule id/name)
- last_decision_at

## Event lifecycle requirements
We need to support:
- events that start as unhandled
- linkage to a handling attempt:
  - handled_by_workflow_execution_id (nullable)
  - handled_at (nullable)
  - handled_status (completed/aborted/failed/paused per semantics)
- idempotency/deduping:
  - optional dedupe_key (pane_id + rule_id + window) to prevent duplicates

Exact column naming is flexible, but the schema must make:
- `wa events --unhandled` cheap
- marking handled a single write

## Deliverables
- SCHEMA string (or migrations folder if we choose migrations now).
- Documented conventions:
  - timestamps: use epoch ms for hot paths (captured_at int)
  - JSON columns are TEXT containing JSON (v0)
  - indexing strategy for common queries

## Testing
- Schema/migration tests (see `wa-4vx.3.10`):
  - create fresh DB
  - upgrade from prior schema versions
  - validate core invariants and indexes exist
- Storage tests (see `wa-4vx.3.7`):
  - insert/query round-trips for segments/gaps/events/workflows

## Acceptance Criteria
- DB initializes successfully on first run.
- Basic inserts into segments/events/panes succeed.
- We can mark an event as handled and query unhandled events efficiently.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.3.10","title":"SQLite migrations & upgrade tests: schema versioning, forward upgrades, invariants","description":"# Task: SQLite migrations & upgrade tests

## Goal
Ensure DB schema evolution is safe and predictable.

Even though we do not care about long-term backwards compatibility, we DO care about:
- wa users upgrading from yesterday's build to today's build
- avoiding "my DB is bricked" situations
- making schema changes intentional and test-backed

## Strategy
- Use an explicit schema version (e.g., `PRAGMA user_version`) and a clear migration runner.
- Keep a minimal set of historical fixtures for N-1 and N-2 schema versions.

## Testing (minimum)
1. Fresh init
   - new empty workspace initializes DB
   - required tables/indexes exist
   - WAL + foreign_keys configured

2. Upgrade path
   - start from an older fixture DB
   - run wa startup migration path
   - verify:
     - user_version bumped correctly
     - no required tables are missing
     - FTS tables/triggers exist and are functional

3. Invariants
   - output_segments `UNIQUE(pane_id, seq)` enforced
   - events lifecycle columns exist and unhandled queries are indexed/fast

## Diagnostics on failure
- Print clear errors:
  - expected vs actual user_version
  - missing table/index names
  - migration step that failed

## Acceptance Criteria
- A contributor can safely change schema by updating migrations AND updating these tests.
- CI catches broken migrations before users do.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.2.9","title":"[Human command] `wa audit` (recent actions feed; filter by pane/workflow; redacted)","description":"# Task: Human command wa audit

## Goal
Expose the audit trail to humans for debugging and trust.

## Features
- wa audit shows recent actions.
- Filters:
  - --pane-id
  - --actor human|robot|mcp|workflow
  - --since / --limit
- Workspace selection:
  - --workspace / WA_WORKSPACE
  - --config (optional)

## Output
- TTY: rich table/panels with severity highlighting for denies/failures.
- Non-TTY: JSONL for piping.

## Safety
- Redaction is always applied.

## Reservation visibility
- Reservation conflicts/denials should be visible with owner + reason + TTL (redacted where needed).

## Testing
- Storage-level tests:
  - covered by `wa-4vx.3.9` (insert/query, redaction, retention)

- CLI contract tests:
  - covered by `wa-nu4.3.2.11` (filter flags, ordering, JSONL stability)

- E2E coverage:
  - policy denial + approval E2E scripts should assert audit rows are emitted with redaction.
  - reservation E2E (`wa-nu4.1.6.4`) should assert audit shows conflict details.

## Logging & debuggability
- `wa audit` should be able to answer “why?” without requiring verbose logs.
- When `--verbose` is enabled, include:
  - resolved workspace/DB path
  - applied filters
  - result count

## Acceptance Criteria
- A user can answer: "Why didn’t wa send text?" by looking at wa audit.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.3","title":"[EPIC] Setup automation: wa setup (wezterm.lua lane, shell integration, SSH hosts)","description":"# [EPIC] Setup & configuration automation

## Mission
Make wa easy to install and hard to misconfigure.

## Why
wa’s correctness depends on:
- having sufficient scrollback / capture strategy
- prompt boundary markers (OSC 133 / user-vars)
- a forwarding lane from WezTerm to wa (wezterm.lua)

Humans should not have to hand-edit large config files or remember magic snippets.

## Scope
- Local setup (`wa setup`): idempotent modifications and installs.
- SSH host discovery for future remote support.

## Non-goals (v0.1)
- Full remote orchestration across machines (Phase 5+)

## Testing & safety (non-negotiable)
Setup is where users decide whether they trust wa. The setup path must be:
- non-destructive
- idempotent
- transparent (shows diffs / planned edits)

Test coverage must include:
- fixture-based unit tests for:
  - ssh config parsing
  - idempotent patching logic (wezterm.lua + shell)

- E2E scripts with artifacts:
  - `wa setup` run twice produces the same result (idempotency)
  - logs and patched files are captured for debugging

## Definition of done
- A new machine can get to a working capture + events pipeline with a single guided `wa setup`.

## Success Criteria
- `wa setup` provides a safe, idempotent path to install wezterm.lua lane and shell integration.
- Setup scripts avoid destructive changes and include clear rollback instructions.
- E2E/manual validation steps exist with detailed logging for troubleshooting.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All items in the following sections are satisfied: Success Criteria, Definition of Done.
- All Testing requirements pass (unit/integration/E2E) with the logging/artifacts described above.
- Any explicit dependencies for this bead are implemented and validated via tests or E2E artifacts.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.3.1","title":"SSH host discovery: parse ~/.ssh/config into structured hosts","description":"# Task: SSH host discovery (parse ~/.ssh/config)

## Goal
Parse `~/.ssh/config` into a structured list of "real" hosts (ignore wildcards) to support:
- future remote domain setup
- human UX (pick a host)

## Requirements
- Ignore wildcard hosts (`*`, `?`).
- Preserve useful fields:
  - Host alias
  - HostName
  - User
  - Port
  - IdentityFile(s)
- Be tolerant of comments/whitespace.

## Safety & privacy
- Do not attempt to read or validate private keys.
- Treat identity file paths as potentially sensitive and redact where appropriate in logs.

## Deliverables
- `SshHost` struct + parser.
- `wa setup --list-hosts` output (can be stubbed if CLI not ready).

## Testing
- Fixture-based unit tests (no real SSH required):
  - representative `~/.ssh/config` fixtures
  - comments/whitespace
  - multiple `Host` stanzas
  - ignored wildcard stanzas
- Ensure stable ordering and deterministic parsing output.

## Acceptance Criteria
- On a representative fixture file, parser returns expected hosts.

-{"id":"wa-nu4.3.3.10","title":"E2E script: wa setup idempotent patching (wezterm.lua + shell) with artifacts","description":"# Task: E2E script — wa setup idempotency

## Goal
Validate that wa setup is truly safe and idempotent.

Setup commands are uniquely user-sensitive: a bug can corrupt dotfiles and destroy trust.
This E2E test provides a safety net with great logging and artifacts.

## Scenario
Run against a temporary fake "home" directory (never touch real dotfiles):
- Provide fixture files:
  - wezterm.lua (various shapes)
  - .zshrc / .bashrc / config.fish fixtures
  - optional ~/.ssh/config fixture

Steps
1) wa setup --dry-run
   - asserts: prints what it would change without writing

2) wa setup (apply mode)
   - asserts: writes only within the temp home

3) wa setup again
   - asserts: no further changes (idempotent)

## Assertions
- wezterm.lua contains exactly one wa-managed block (WA-BEGIN/WA-END) after apply.
- Running again makes zero changes.
- Shell integration is installed once and is idempotent.
- Output is verbose, timestamped, and never contains secrets.

## Artifacts
- before/after copies of each modified file
- unified diff outputs
- full command logs

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Acceptance Criteria
- The test is deterministic and safe to run in CI.
- Failures clearly identify which patcher step is not idempotent.


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Add an explicit guard that the test never writes outside the temp home (assert all modified paths are under the temp root).
  - Include at least one “hostile fixture” variant (wezterm.lua already contains a partial WA block / malformed markers) to ensure patcher behavior is safe.

+{"id":"wa-nu4.3.3.10","title":"E2E script: wa setup idempotent patching (wezterm.lua + shell) with artifacts","description":"# Task: E2E script — wa setup idempotency

## Goal
Validate that wa setup is truly safe and idempotent.

Setup commands are uniquely user-sensitive: a bug can corrupt dotfiles and destroy trust.
This E2E test provides a safety net with great logging and artifacts.

## Scenario
Run against a temporary fake "home" directory (never touch real dotfiles):
- Provide fixture files:
  - wezterm.lua (various shapes)
  - .zshrc / .bashrc / config.fish fixtures
  - optional ~/.ssh/config fixture

Steps
1) wa setup --dry-run
   - asserts: prints what it would change without writing

2) wa setup (apply mode)
   - asserts: writes only within the temp home

3) wa setup again
   - asserts: no further changes (idempotent)

## Assertions
- wezterm.lua contains exactly one wa-managed block (WA-BEGIN/WA-END) after apply.
- Running again makes zero changes.
- Shell integration is installed once and is idempotent.
- Output is verbose, timestamped, and never contains secrets.

## Artifacts
- before/after copies of each modified file
- unified diff outputs
- full command logs

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Acceptance Criteria
- The test is deterministic and safe to run in CI.
- Failures clearly identify which patcher step is not idempotent.


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Add an explicit guard that the test never writes outside the temp home (assert all modified paths are under the temp root).
  - Include at least one “hostile fixture” variant (wezterm.lua already contains a partial WA block / malformed markers) to ensure patcher behavior is safe.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.3.11","title":"E2E harness: wa setup remote against dockerized sshd (artifacts, rollback safety)","description":"# Task: E2E harness — wa setup remote

## Goal
Provide a repeatable E2E test for wa setup remote without requiring real servers.

Remote setup is high-risk (it touches:
- remote files
- systemd user services
- installs binaries/configs)

An E2E harness helps us ship confidently.

## Approach
- Use a dockerized sshd container as the "remote host".
- Run wa setup remote against it with:
  - dry-run mode
  - apply mode
  - idempotency verification (run twice)

## Safety constraints
- The harness must guarantee it only touches the container filesystem.
- Always run with verbose logging and produce artifacts.

## Assertions
- Remote install steps are idempotent.
- systemd user service unit file is created/updated as expected.
- Logs include clear step start/end markers and show any rollback path taken.

## Artifacts
- captured stdout/stderr
- remote filesystem snapshot (or tarball of touched paths)
- systemd unit contents

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Acceptance Criteria
- The harness runs locally and in CI (where supported) without flakes.


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Assert the harness refuses to run if the “remote host” is not clearly the docker container (avoid accidental real-host modification).
  - Add a failure-injection step (e.g., simulate missing package manager) and ensure rollback reporting is captured in artifacts.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.3.12","title":"Decide default scrollback_lines + setup/doctor guidance","description":"# Task: Decide default scrollback_lines + setup/doctor guidance

## Goal
Resolve the open question: what scrollback depth gives reliable capture without excessive memory usage.

## Why
wa depends on scrollback for capture fidelity. Too low → gaps and missed detections; too high → memory bloat.
We need a documented default and clear warnings.

## Work items
- Benchmark memory/CPU impact for a range (e.g., 10k / 50k / 100k lines) on typical machines.
- Choose a default (tentative: 50k) and document the tradeoffs.
- Update config defaults and `wa setup` guidance.
- Update `wa doctor` to warn when scrollback is below the recommended minimum.

## Deliverables
- Decision note in this bead (final default + rationale).
- Config default in `wa.toml` schema.
- Setup/doctor output reflects the chosen recommendation.

## Testing
- Unit tests for the doctor warning threshold.
- Setup dry-run output includes the recommended value.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Default scrollback_lines is chosen and documented.
- Users get actionable warnings when their config is below the minimum.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.3.2","title":"Idempotent wezterm.lua patch: user-var forwarding lane to wa","description":"# Task: Idempotent `wezterm.lua` patch (user-var forwarding lane)

## Goal
Automate installation of a small `wezterm.lua` snippet that forwards wa-related `user-var-changed` events to wa.

## Why
- OSC 133 markers are good, but user-vars give us an extra deterministic channel.
- Forwarding is necessary for future features (explicit checkpoints, prompt boundaries, "agent ready" signals).
- Humans should not need to copy/paste Lua.

## Minimal forwarding lane (PLAN Appendix E.1)
This is the exact minimal Lua we must be able to install idempotently:

```lua
-- Forward user-var events to wa daemon
wezterm.on('user-var-changed', function(window, pane, name, value)
  if name:match('^wa%-') then
    wezterm.background_child_process {
      'wa', 'event', '--from-uservar',
      '--pane', tostring(pane:pane_id()),
      '--name', name,
      '--value', value
    }
  end
end)
```

Notes:
- Prefix filter: default is `wa-` (hyphen). If we later support `wa_`, the filter must remain strict and configurable.
- The background child process must be non-blocking for WezTerm UI responsiveness.

## Approach
- `wa setup` locates the active `wezterm.lua`.
- It applies an idempotent patch:
  - insert snippet if missing
  - do nothing if already present
- It creates a backup copy (non-destructive).

## Forwarding lane contract
- We only forward vars matching the configured prefix.
- The Lua code calls:
  - `wa event --from-uservar --pane <id> --name <name> --value <value>`
- wa decodes base64 payloads where applicable and emits an internal signal/event.

## Deliverables
- Lua snippet template (managed block with WA-BEGIN/WA-END markers).
- WezTerm config locator.
- Idempotent patcher.

## Testing
- Unit/fixture tests (see `wa-nu4.3.3.5`):
  - patcher inserts exactly one managed block
  - re-running is a no-op
  - backups are created and paths are reported
- E2E:
  - `wa-nu4.3.3.10` validates end-to-end idempotency in a temp HOME
  - `wa-4vx.10.14` validates user-var events flow through the forwarding lane

## Acceptance Criteria
- Re-running `wa setup` does not duplicate the snippet.
- Manual review of the file shows only the intended additions.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.3.6","title":"Remote setup spec: wezterm install, mux server service, wa install, safety/rollback","description":"# Task: Remote setup spec (`wa setup remote`)

## Goal
Define a robust, user-friendly remote bootstrap flow that makes WezTerm domains reliable:
- install WezTerm if missing
- run `wezterm-mux-server` as a user systemd service
- enable linger so the mux survives logout
- optionally install `wa` on the remote

## Why
A core user journey in the plan is:
> “Bring a new remote host online: install matching WezTerm, set up mux server, standardize config.”

## Safety requirements
- Must be **explicit** and non-surprising:
  - default to `--dry-run`
  - require confirmation for any operation that changes the remote host
- Must be **idempotent**:
  - re-run safely
  - detect existing installs/services
- Must be **observable**:
  - verbose logging and step timing
  - clear final summary with next steps

## Plan (proposed)
1. Choose host (from SSH config or explicit `--host`).
2. Verify connectivity.
3. Detect WezTerm presence + version.
4. If missing:
   - detect package manager (apt/dnf)
   - install WezTerm (documented script)
5. Install systemd user service unit for `wezterm-mux-server`.
6. `systemctl --user enable --now wezterm-mux-server`.
7. `loginctl enable-linger $USER` (requires sudo; explain).
8. Verify service is active.
9. Optionally scp `wa` binary to `~/.local/bin/wa`.

## Deliverables
- A written contract for:
  - CLI flags
  - what remote commands we run
  - what files we create
  - what errors we surface

## Testing
- Fixture-based tests:
  - covered by `wa-nu4.3.3.9` (dry-run plan output stability, command rendering, logging)

- E2E coverage:
  - covered by `wa-nu4.3.3.11` (dockerized sshd harness + artifacts + rollback safety)

## Acceptance Criteria
- A reviewer can implement remote setup from this issue without re-reading `PLAN.md`.
- The spec includes a rollback story (how to disable service / undo).

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.3.7","title":"[Human command] `wa setup remote` (ssh install, systemd user service, verify, verbose logs)","description":"# Task: Implement `wa setup remote`

## Goal
Implement the remote setup subcommand described in `wa-nu4.3.3.6`.

## Requirements
- Must support `--dry-run` and `--yes`/confirmation.
- Must emit detailed logs (including per-step durations).
- Must be resilient:
  - handles already-installed wezterm
  - handles existing systemd service
  - surfaces actionable errors (missing sudo, unsupported OS/package manager)

## Deliverables
- Remote command runner wrappers (`ssh`, `scp`) with:
  - timeouts
  - stdout/stderr capture
  - redaction for any accidental secrets
- Remote install flows:
  - apt-based
  - dnf-based
- Service unit installation using an embedded template.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Running against a test host:
  - installs/ensures wezterm
  - starts mux service
  - verifies active
  - prints a clear summary with follow-up steps


## Testing
- E2E tests:
  - Use the dockerized sshd harness (`wa-nu4.3.3.11`) to validate:
    - dry-run does not modify the host
    - apply mode installs/starts service
    - idempotent second run makes no further changes
    - failures produce artifact bundles (remote stdout/stderr, transcripts)

- Safety tests:
  - Add a “rollback safety” scenario: if a step fails mid-way, ensure the command reports what may have changed and what to do next.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.4","title":"[EPIC] Diagnostic Bundle + Crash Capture","description":"# [EPIC] Diagnostic Bundle + Crash Capture

## Mission
Make it easy to answer "Is wa healthy?" and "Why did it do that?" without guesswork, including crash-only diagnostics.

## Deliverables
- `wa doctor` checks (environment + config + DB)
- health snapshot plumbing from the watcher
- diagnostic bundle export (sanitized)
- crash bundle capture + listing/triage integration
- performance budget checks in CI

## Deferred
- Incident/reproduce workflows are deferred until demand (see wa-upg.1).

## Testing & observability (non-negotiable)
Diagnostics tooling is only valuable if it is reliable and safe.

- Unit/integration tests must verify:
  - doctor checks against fixtures (no real WezTerm required)
  - diagnostic/crash bundle contents and layout
  - redaction: bundles/logs never leak secrets

- E2E scripts must verify:
  - `wa doctor` behaves correctly in healthy vs broken scenarios
  - crashes produce actionable messages and point to artifacts

## Success Criteria
- `wa doctor`/`wa status` provide actionable health signals (DB connectivity, queue depths, ingest lag, lock state).
- Diagnostic/crash bundles include logs, effective config, and redacted diagnostics for bug reports.
- Performance budgets exist for core hot paths and regressions are visible in CI.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.4.1","title":"[Human command] `wa doctor` (environment + config + DB checks)","description":"# Task: Human command wa doctor

## Goal
Provide a deterministic checklist that validates the wa environment.

Doctor is a user trust surface: it must make failures actionable and help users understand where wa stores state.

## Checks (initial)
- Workspace/config resolution
  - print resolved workspace root
  - print resolved DB/log/lock/socket paths
  - verify workspace is writable

- WezTerm
  - wezterm CLI available (wezterm cli list works)
  - wezterm version meets minimum supported
  - scrollback settings are sufficient (warn if too low; best-effort)

- Storage
  - DB is readable/writable
  - schema/migration version matches
  - WAL + foreign_keys configured

- Watcher status (if running)
  - last tick time
  - tailer count
  - backlog sizes

- Feature availability
  - browser (playwright)
  - mcp
  - web/tui/sync (optional)

## Output
- Rich panels when TTY.
- Non-TTY JSON suitable for automation.

## Safety
- Output must never include secrets (even if config contains tokens).
- Redaction must be applied to any potentially sensitive field.

## Testing
- Unit/integration tests: `wa-nu4.3.4.8` (fixture-based checks + output stability)
- E2E: `wa-4vx.10.22` (healthy/broken scenarios with verbose artifacts)

## Acceptance Criteria
- On a healthy system, wa doctor exits 0 with a concise summary.
- On a broken system, it exits non-zero and lists actionable fixes.
- Output never includes secrets (even if config contains tokens).

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.4.2","title":"Expose watcher health snapshot in CLI (status/health command)","description":"# Task: Expose watcher health snapshot

## Goal
Make the watcher’s health state visible so we can quickly diagnose:
- stuck tailers
- slow polling
- DB backpressure
- pattern match overload

## Metrics (example)
- discovery tick duration
- tailer count + per-domain breakdown
- per-pane last_seen + last_seq
- storage write queue depth
- event bus queue depths
- FTS insert latency

## Deliverables
- `wa status --health` (or `wa health`) prints the snapshot.
- Robot/MCP can access the same snapshot if useful.

## Testing
- Unit/integration tests:
  - health snapshot JSON is schema-parseable and stable
  - “stuck pane” synthetic scenario is surfaced clearly

- CLI contract tests:
  - covered by `wa-nu4.3.2.11` (stable JSON output in non-TTY mode)

- Diagnostic coverage:
  - `wa doctor` tests (`wa-nu4.3.4.8`) should validate that health signals are consistent with watcher state when available.

## Acceptance Criteria
- When a pane stops producing output, health snapshot highlights it clearly.
